questions.question: What does a strong chat answer clarify before discussing WebSockets or queues?
options: Whether the service promises durable send acknowledgment, ordering, and offline replay.
Which icon set the mobile client uses for typing indicators.
Whether SQL is forbidden in the design.
How many emojis each message should support.
questions.explanation: Delivery semantics are the real contract of the system.

questions.question: Why are client-generated message IDs useful in chat systems?
options: They make all messages smaller over the wire.
They help the server dedupe retries when mobile clients resend after uncertainty.
They eliminate the need for a conversation log.
They force every message to be globally ordered.
questions.explanation: Retries are common, and dedupe needs a stable operation identifier.

questions.question: Why is acknowledging a send after durable append often a good default?
options: It gives the sender a clear contract without requiring every recipient device to be online immediately.
It guarantees every recipient has already read the message.
It removes the need for replay cursors.
It makes presence heartbeats unnecessary.
questions.explanation: Durable append separates correctness from real-time delivery variability.

questions.question: What role should presence usually play in chat delivery?
options: It should be treated as a routing hint rather than the sole source of delivery correctness.
It should fully replace durable inboxes.
It should decide message authorization by itself.
It should guarantee globally synchronized clocks.
questions.explanation: Presence is helpful but too fragile to be the only correctness mechanism.

questions.question: Which metric best captures user-visible recovery after disconnects?
options: Replay latency from reconnect to catch-up completion.
Average keyboard type-ahead length.
Profile image upload size.
Total employees on the chat team.
questions.explanation: Reconnect recovery is a first-class user outcome for chat.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with waiting for recipient-device acknowledgment before showing 'sent'?
options: It provides a stronger signal but makes sender-perceived latency and failure sensitivity much worse.
It lowers backend cost because no durable log is needed.
It prevents duplicate messages automatically.
It simplifies offline sync for all devices.
questions.explanation: The sender experience now depends on every downstream delivery hop.

questions.question: Why should a chat backend separate online fanout from offline replay?
options: Because low-latency push and durable catch-up have different performance and storage needs.
Because only one device per user is ever active.
Because replay makes ordering impossible.
Because presence storage cannot coexist with sockets.
questions.explanation: These are related but distinct paths with different constraints.

questions.question: What is the most realistic ordering promise for many chat systems?
options: Per-conversation ordering with client-side dedupe and replay handling.
Global ordering across every conversation in all regions.
No ordering at all because chat is best effort.
Physical-clock ordering across offline devices without conflicts.
questions.explanation: Local ordering where it matters is usually the right contract.
